title: قرار رقم 76 لسنة 2017: بشأن اعتماد سياسة الخدمات الشمولية

provisions.content: of Federal Law by Decree No. (3) of 2003, as amended, Regarding theOrganization of the Telecommunications Sector (the “ TelecommunicationsLaw”) establishes the Telecommunications Regulatory Authority (the“TRA”) as the competent body to oversee the telecommunicationssector in the UAE.1.2

of the Telecommunications Law, the Telecommunications Servicesprovided throughout the UAE are sufficient to satisfy the needs of those whowish to make use of such services; that these Telecommunications Services areenhanced in order to promote the interests of subscribers; encourage, promote,and develop the telecommunications and information technology industries in theState, and promote and enhance the telecommunications system in the Statethrough ensuring the availability of the latest facilities provided bytelecommunications technology.3.10In addition and with this Policy, the TRA also seeks to meet the needs of theConsumers and the UAE more generally. The Vision of the Federal Government hasset the key themes for socio-economic development for the country and has at itsheart the objective of making, by 2021, the UAE one of the best countries in theworld in which to live. This translates into concrete objectives for the mainsectors of the economy, including telecommunications, with the specificobjectives that in this sector the UAE is:- to be ranked among the top 10 countries by 2021, based on a NetworkedReadinessIndex(measured by the World Economic Forum); an index which captures (among otherfactors) the availability of infrastructure, usage of ICT and the economic andsocial impact of the telecommunications and ICT sector-to be ranked fisrt on the Online Service Index, which focuses on the evolutionof e-government services (smart services) in terms of availability, quality,connectivity and diversity of channels and the use by the public of theseservices.3.11Wide availability of Telecommunications Services (in particular, ultrafast fixedbroadband and high-speed mobile broadband) is clearly key to achieving theseobjectives.3.12The TRA therefore is introducing measures to ensure the universal availabilityof, and accessibility to, a minimum set of Telecommunications Servicessufficient to support the above mentioned objectives. These shall addressspecifically access to high speed data and other Telecommunications Servicesmore generally in sparsely-populated areas. This document therefore sets out theTRA’s Universal Services (US) policy, on which any further necessaryregulatory measures shall be based.4-Definition of Universal Service4.1In this section, the scope of the proposed Universal Service Policy is definedin terms of the services that should be included and the areas where theseservices should be provided.4.2As a general principle, the TRA recognises the importance of ensuring thatConsumers throughout the UAE have access to the same minimum set of services, atthe same price, regardless of their location. In practice, this means that allConsumers in Permanent Dwellings should have access to services which arecapable of delivering basic voice, TV services, and high speed data packages ofat least10Mbps .Furthermore, this suggests that as services are upgraded in urban areas, theseupgraded services should also be made available throughout the UAE. As such, theTRA would expect the minimum broadband speed required under this Policy toincrease in line with increases in the average speed for the majority ofconsumers in the UAE.4.3This principle will mean that:-A Licensee who has an obligation to provide Universal Services must satisfy anyrequest by Consumers residing in a Permanent Dwelling to deliver the UniversalServices, as defined by the TRA, which shall include those described above at auniform standard price; and-A Licensee who has an obligation to provide Universal Services in a particulararea must continue to provide the Universal Services described above to allConsumers in that area, together with all Public Organisations in that area, atuniform standard prices, unless that Consumer voluntarily seeks to disconnectits service or the service is legally terminated say for failure to payoutstanding invoices.4.4However, extending high-speed broadband coverage to so-called ‘uneconomicareas’ would also impose costs on the telecommunications industry (this isdiscussed in detail in Section 5 below). Therefore, it is important to ensurethat any Universal Service Obligation is delivered in an efficient way whichmaximises net benefits to society (i.e. total additional benefits net ofadditional costs).4.5The benefits to society of this Universal Service Policy are likely to bemaximised if the advanced broadband services are delivered by a technologycapable of ultrafast speeds and characterised by high resilience and highQuality of Service (QoS). While there are several potential technologies whichcould satisfy these criteria, fibre (FTTH) stands out due to its future proofnature (as it is capable of delivering speeds of 1Gb/s and above). Therefore,the TRA’s preference is that fibre (FTTH) networks should, where feasible,be deployed to all Consumers and Public Organisations in the UAE. This has theadvantage that once a Licensee deploys its fibre network, there is no reason whythat Licensee could not offer higher data speeds to its customers, meaning thatas Telecommunications Services are enhanced in one part of the UAE, theincremental cost of offering the same Telecommunications Services in the rest ofthe UAE is likely to be limited. In areas where there are physical constraintson fibre deployment (for example, where surfaces are not levelled), acombination of other technologies – fixed wireless access and satellite– may be used as long as they satisfy the USO definition (set out above),continue to do so in the future (as the USO speed requirement increases overtime) and are approved by the TRA as part of the Universal ServiceImplementation Plan which shall be developed in accordance with thisPolicy.5-Mechanisms for implementing the Universal Service Policy; the process foridentifying the Universal Service Provider(s)5.1In order to ensure this Universal Service Policy is implemented and itsobjectives are met, the TRA will issue a subsequent instrument to designate oneor more Licensee as having an obligation to provide Universal Services in theUAE. This provider or providers shall then be known as the Universal ServiceProvider (USP) and shall be obligated to meet the needs arising from theUniversal Service Obligation (USO) placed upon it.5.2Given the scope of the Universal Service Policy described in the previoussection, in designating a USP the TRA will take into account, inter alia, theexisting networks of the Licensees and their geographical coverage, whilst itwill also consider whether one Licensee is in a position to extend its existingnetworks significantly more quickly, and at lower cost, than otherproviders.5.3In some cases the USP, if appropriate, may be allowed to subcontract to otherLicensees the provision of some of the Telecommunications Services which it isobliged to supply under its USO. Such circumstances may arise if – by wayof example – broadband services in some areas need to be provided throughsatellite, in which case the USP could enter into an agreement with a Licenseeholding an appropriate licence to offer, on behalf of the USP, the relevantservices. Any such arrangements will, however, need to be approved by the TRAbefore being implemented.5.4For the avoidance of doubt, the USP will not by its USP status be entitled toexclusivity in any particular area that it may be obliged to cover. Thus, ifanother Licensee is planning to roll out telecommunication services in an areacurrently not being covered (e.g. in a new settlement), it will not be precludedfrom doing so by virtue of the fact that a USP is already covering itFurthermore, any obligations imposed by the TRA on Licensees to provide networkaccess services nationally, such as bitstream access, shall also apply in areaswhere network coverage has been expanded as a result of the USO placed on theUSP.5.5Following the USP’s designation, it shall be required to work with theTRA, local Government municipalities and such other entities as may be specifiedby the TRA to develop a Universal Service Implementation Plan to satisfy theparameters of this Policy. This Plan shall include the services, geographicalareas and the technologies which shall be used by to provide Universal Servicesand the planned delivery times. It shall also include all such details andprocedures necessary to ensure its effective implementation. The Plan must becompleted and submitted to the TRA for approval within [12 months] of theLicensee’s designation as the USP.6-Universal Service funding6.1The TRA recognises that the USP is likely to face a net cost in meeting the USO,as a result of the costs it will incur in deploying and maintaining networkinfrastructure in areas where otherwise it might have not rolled out itsnetwork. Depending on the scale of this net cost it may be appropriate tocompensate the USP for the costs incurred in relation to its fulfilment of theUSO. The source of the funds from which such costs may be covered is referred toas USO funding.Fundingmechanism6.2There are three potential approaches to USO funding:-Self-funding by the USO provider;-Industry funding; and-Government subsidy6.3The TRA recognises that each of these methods could be appropriate under certaincircumstances. For example:-Self-funding by the USO provider – this can be appropriate if the cost ofthe USO is immaterial or the USP does not face effective competition in themarket. This is because in the latter case, the USP can cross-subsidise the USOcosts through the returns it makes on serving other Customers or providing otherTelecommunications Services. However, if the USP faces effective competitionfrom other Licensees, cross-subsidisation might be prohibited or may not bepossible as profits in low cost areas will be competed away.-Industry funding – the net costs of the USO may also be met through anindustry fund, with contributions coming from some or all market participants,for example in proportion to their revenue market shares.Thismechanism has been used extensively to fund fixed telephony Universal Servicepolicies in many countries around the world. This means that the net costs ofmeeting the USO are shared by the industry (rather than levied on one provider).However, industry funds may be complex to set up and administer and may evencreate market distortions compared to government funding, as typically suchcontribution requirements do not apply to the over-the-top and othernon-traditional telecommunications players who may access the networks.-Government subsidy (grant) – the USO net costs may also be covered bygovernment through a subsidy. This may be appropriate if the USO net cost issignificant, and therefore could otherwise place an unfair burden on the USP. Inthis context, it is critical to ensure that any public subsidy does not displaceprivate investment and is not used to place the USP at an advantageous positionover other Licensees. The costs of the USP (to be compensated either bygovernment or by industry funding) need to be scrutinised by the regulator toavoid over-recovery.6.4The TRA shall at an appropriate time following the completion of the UniversalService Implementation Plan, publish an appropriate instrument setting out,inter alia, in detail how the USO shall be funded, when and how the USP mayapply for the relevant funds etc., building on the principales described in thissection of the Policy. However, unless it can be shown that the USO creates anunfair burden on the USP then the TRA would expect that the USO should be fundedby the USP, such as from the returns it earns in offering other services in theUAE.6.5If the net cost of meeting its USO is material and risks placing an unfairburden on the USP, the USP will be able to apply for additional funding. In itsapplication, it will have to provide calculations of the USO net costs (based onthe methodology discussed below and which will be set out in more detail in theregulatory instruments to be issued pursuant to this Regulatory Policy). The TRAwould review the USP’s applicat

(3) of the Telecommunications Law specifically grants to the TRA thepower to issue regulations, instructions, decisions and rules regulatingUniversal Service.1.4Currently, the TRA’s policy for promoting the universal provision ofTelecommunications Services is captured in a number of different regulatoryinstruments. However, to date, there has been no Universal Service vision orPolicy statement for the telecommunications sector of the UAE. This RegulatoryPolicy therefore sets out the TRA’s vision, rationale and policy frameworkfor introducing and implementing a Universal Service Policy for the sector,taking into account the existing legal and regulatory framework as well asapplicable UAE Government Policies.1.5In due time the TRA, where necessary, may issue other appropriate regulatoryinstruments so as to implement or facilitate the implementation of the specificpolicy measures set out herein. This Regulatory Policy does not replaceUniversal Service provisions currently set out in other legal or regulatory TRAinstruments, such as, without limitation, the Consumer Protection Regulations of2015 or the Regulatory Policy on Directory Information of 2009.TheStructure of this document1.6The remainder of this Regulatory Policy isstructured as follows:-Section 2 defines terms used in this Regulatory Policy.-Section 3 sets out the TRA’s vision and rationale for introducing aUniversal Services Policy.-Section 4 defines the scope of the Universal Service Obligation (USO) both interms of the services included and in terms of the areas that shall becovered.-Section 5 sets out the TRA’s position on how the USO policy will beimplemented, including a mechanism for choosing a USO Provider.-Section 6 addresses the issues of USO funding.-Section 7 addresses the issues of affordability and accessibility of theTelecommunications Services in the UAE.2-Definitions2.1All words, terms and phrases used in this Regulatory Policy shall have themeanings ascribed to them, if any, in the Telecommunications Law, unlessotherwise provided for in this Policy. The following terms and phrases shallhave the meanings ascribed to them below when used in this Policy:Word,term or phraseDefinitionConsumerAnyperson, whether a natural person or a legal entity but excluding a Licensee,which person consumes or may consume a Public Telecommunications Serviceprovided by a Licensee.UniversalService (US)Theprovision of a defined minimum set of Telecommunications Services to allConsumers at an affordable price.UniversalService Implementation PlanThePlan to be developed by the Universal Service Provider in accordance with thisPolicy.UniversalService Obligation (USO)AnObligation imposed on a Licensee to provide Universal Services.UniversalService Provider (USP)ALincensee determined by the TRA to implement a Universal ServiceObligationUniversalService PolicyThisRegulatory Instrument, wherein the TRA sets out its vision, rationale and policyframework for introducing and implementing a Universal Service Policy for thesector and prescribes the method(s) by which it proposes to achieve these.PermanentDwellingsPermanentDwelling shall mean any accommodation, regularly occupied by a person or personsto live in permanently.PublicOrganisationsForthe purposes of the Universal Service Policy, public organisations includegovernment entities, and government approved schools and healthcarefacilities3-The TRA’s Vision, Policy Direction and Objectives3.1In recent years, Licensees in the telecommunications sector have already madesignificant progress in providing Telecommunications Services (both basic andadvanced) to Consumers in the UAE.3.2Licensees have deployed advanced network infrastructures and as a result, theUAE is a global leader in terms of fibre broadband availability and take up,having 75% of households connected to fibrebroadband networks and over 90% of homes passed byfibre .3.3The fibre broadband networks have been rolled out by two Licensees in the UAE -Etisalat and du. Etisalat’s fibre network covers the majority ofpopulated areas in the country, while du has rolled out its fibre network innewly developed areas of Dubai. Since October 2015, both Licensees have beenable to offer services to customers through reciprocal bitstream access, givingConsumers a choice of Licensee for their fixed line voice and broadbandservices.3.4Both Etisalat and du use these networks to offer triple-play packages, bundlingfixed telephony, broadband and TV services. Take up of multi-play packages issignificant – at the end of 2015, 23% and 61% of Etisalat and du’sfixed broadband subscribers in the UAE had double-play and triple-play bundles,respectively .3.5Despite these positive trends, the TRA is concerned that a number of Consumerswithin the UAE do not have access to the same level and quality ofTelecommunications Services as Consumers in the main population centres. This islargely driven by the fact that rolling out infrastructure in some ruralharder-to-reach areas is more challenging, both financially and practically thanelsewhere in the country. As a result, this has meant that infrastructure hasnot been deployed universally across the UAE to the same standard, therebydepriving Consumers in such areas of the same level of services that thoseliving in urban areas can enjoy.3.6The TRA recognises that this is not in accordance with its mandates under theTelecommunications Law and Government policies that require that no citizen isexcluded from the opportunities afforded by high quality access toTelecommunications Services.3.7Indeed, access to Telecommunications Services, including today high-speedbroadband, is no longer regarded as a luxury and has arguably become anecessity. This means that Consumers should not be denied access toTelecommunications Services based on their geographic location, particularlygiven that more and more information and services are delivered online, such asInternet banking, e-health, e-commerce, online learning, e-government. To dootherwise would not promote fairness and social inclusion.3.8In addition, there is a considerable body of evidence demonstrating that havingaccess to high-speed broadband productivity, improves labour participation(through remote working), promotes SME creation, and ultimately contributes toeconomic development and growth. This is likely to be particularly important forrural areas, where opportunities for economic development and growth mightotherwise be more limited than in the main population centres of the UAE.3.9In light of the above and the Federal Government’s Vision 2021 for theUAE, the TRA has developed this Policy so as to ensure that, in line with

of the Telecommunications Law.)8-Effective Date8.1This Policy shall be effective on the date of issue.9-Revocation9.1The TRA may revise, revoke, or reissue this Policy from time to time as it seesfit.[1] -Information provided by Licensees[2] -The FTTH Council 2017[3] -https://www.tra.gov.ae/en/open-data/annual-market-review.aspx[4]This is a composite indicator that measures the readiness of a country tocapture opportunities provided by information and communication technology toincrease competitiveness based on (4) perspectives: Environment (the regulatoryenvironment for information and communication technology and the environment forbusiness and innovation), Readiness (Infrastructure for information andcommunication technology and availability of expertise/skills), Usage (use ofICT by individuals, businesses, and government), Impact (the economic and socialimpact of information and communication technology).[5] -For the avoidance of doubt, access to basic voice services shall include accessto free calls to emergency service numbers (i.e., public police, fire, andambulance, and other emergency services) and other voice services, as designatedby the TRA.
